checked.title: Finding 1: Saturation-based stopping for iterative extraction (plateau by iteration ~3)
checked.note: PRIMARY SOURCE FULLY VERIFIED. Fetched https://arxiv.org/html/2404.04068 ('Assessing the quality of information extraction'): all quoted numbers and passages check out — 'From the third iteration, i.e., after four LLM calls, the majority of scores... are either getting worse or stagnating'; Table 2 semantic similarity peaks 0.7572 at iter 3 then declines; bias-avoidance degrades 0.5614->0.4247; explicit recommendation to 'stop when additional LLM call will return no or only a few new entities'; iteration count scales with text length. HOWEVER, the secondary omega-rule attribution FAILS: fetched arXiv 2511.00592 in full ('Agentic Auto-Scheduling: An Experimental Study of LLM-Guided Loop Optimization') — it is about COMPILER loop optimization (ComPilot, PolyBench speedups), and contains NO omega/marginal-gain early-stopping rule; its only stopping mechanism is the LLM self-issuing 'no_further_transformations'. The finding correctly hedged this as an unfetched lead, and the lead is a dead end AS CITED. The omega rule t_stop = min{t : Delta_xt(S_{t-1}) <= omega * Delta_x1(S_0)} DOES exist verbatim — but in SPICE, arXiv 2601.23155 (submodular data selection for LLM TRAINING, SPICE+ with omega=0.5), verified by downloading and grepping the PDF. DOWNGRADE: re-attribute the omega rule to arXiv 2601.23155 and re-label its domain as submodular training-data selection, not '2025-26 agentic-loop work'. The headline saturation claim stands.

checked.title: Finding 2: Variable-cardinality moment retrieval (GMR) with set-completeness metrics
checked.note: Fetched https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02623 ('Retrieving Any Relevant Moments: Benchmark and Models for Generalized Moment Retrieval', submitted May 4 2026): abstract verbatim confirms 'retrieve the complete set of relevant moments or predicting an empty set', Soccer-GMR benchmark, the exact quoted metrics wording ('complementary metrics tailored for null-set rejection, positive-query localization, and end-to-end GMR performance'), the lightweight plug-and-play adapter + GMR-tailored GRPO reward paradigms, and code/dataset release. The OpenReview link is behind a bot-verification wall, but the ICLR 2025 paper is independently confirmed via iclr.cc virtual poster 28243 and the ICLR 2025 proceedings: 'Generalized Video Moment Retrieval' (GVMR) with non-target and multi-target queries, NExT-VMR dataset, BCANet baseline — published as a conference paper at ICLR 2025. CAVEATS: (a) that Soccer-GMR explicitly 'extends' the ICLR 2025 paper was not verifiable from the abstract (plausible given shared task name, but unconfirmed); (b) 'the field is abandoning fixed-K' is editorial extrapolation from two papers — downgrade to 'explicit precedent exists'. Soccer-GMR verification is abstract-level only; baseline numbers/protocol details not independently inspected.

checked.title: Finding 3: HiVid continuous LLM saliency scoring with sliding-window batching
checked.note: STRONGEST FINDING. Fetched full HTML of https://arxiv.org/html/2602.14214 ('HiVid: LLM-Guided Video Saliency For Content-Aware VOD And Live Streaming'). All nine sub-claims verified with exact text/table matches: 'scalable human proxy' (abstract), window m=10 with periodic summary (Sec 3.2/4), LLM-guided merge-sort re-ranking (abstract), [0,1] normalized chunk weights with no fixed highlight count (Sec 3.3), PLCC 0.66/0.50/0.47 on YouTube-8M/TVSum/SumMe with 11.5% avg improvement (Table 1), mAP@50 0.86 (YouTube-8M), stable sensitive-content scores (Table 7, politics PLCC 0.73 std 0.05), open models Llama-3.2 0.58 / Qwen3-VL 0.60 / InternVL3 0.64, and Table 2 cost $8.12 vs $1.35 for the 201s GPT-4o video. TWO SMALL PRECISION DOWNGRADES: (a) the $8.12 baseline is window m=2, NOT unbatched single-frame calls — so it is a m=2-vs-m=10 comparison, and '6x cut from batching' slightly understates what batching does but the framing 'unbatched -> batched' would overstate the measured baseline; state it as 'm=2 -> m=10'; (b) the paper reports '11.5% and 6% improvement on average PLCC' against two comparison sets, so '+11.5% over next best' may conflate which baseline; safer phrasing is '+11.5% average PLCC improvement'.

checked.title: Finding 4: Batch prompting makes judge cost sublinear (30-82% cost cuts, ~3pp loss, reasoning-token collapse)
checked.note: MOSTLY VERIFIED BUT CONTAINS ONE FLAT MISATTRIBUTION, so the claim AS STATED is not fully backed. Verified: (1) arXiv 2406.10786 fetched — exact quotes confirm 75.5%->72.3% ('a minor 3.2% absolute drop') across 7 LLMs / 6 benchmarks and cost reductions 'from 30.7% to 82.0%'; but the '~8-16 batch-size stability threshold' is the finding-writer's gloss — the paper says accuracy generally declines with task size, degradation pronounced at 20+, and LLMs stay >=90% of single-item accuracy 'most of the time'; soften to that. (2) arXiv 2511.04108 fetched in HTML ('Reasoning Under Constraint: How Batch Prompting Suppresses Overthinking in Reasoning Models') — confirms 2,950 -> 710 reasoning tokens at batch size 15, but the paper calls it a 76% reduction (= 4.15x, not '4.5x'; correct the multiplier), pooled over 13 benchmarks on DeepSeek-R1 and OpenAI-o1, 'preserving or improving accuracy'. (3) RoBatch (arXiv 2605.28268, 'Towards Cost-effective LLMs Routing with Batch Prompting', May 27 2026) — abstract verbatim confirms Route-with-Batching, batch-aware proxy utility model, greedy Pareto scheduling, Qwen3/Gemma3, six benchmarks. FALSE AS ATTRIBUTED: the claim that the medRxiv screening study found 'batch size 15 IMPROVED accuracy 75.0%->78.2%'. I downloaded the actual medRxiv PDF (2025.09.02.25334159, 'Scaling the Prompt...', 29 pp) and grepped it: 75.0/78.2/batch-15 appear NOWHERE. Those exact numbers are the accuracy result of arXiv 2511.04108 (accuracy rises 75.0%->78.2% at batch size 15) — classic number-laundering across search summaries. The screening study is real and directionally supportive in a different way: batches of 1-790 records with Gemini 2.5 Pro/Flash, GPT-5/GPT-5 mini; sensitivity/specificity (not accuracy) mostly stable up to 200+; Gemini 2.5 Flash sensitivity IMPROVED from batch 1 (0.85) with batching, while GPT-5 mini degraded 0.88->0.48 from batch 200 to 400. DOWNGRADE: strike the medRxiv numeric sentence, replace with the sensitivity/specificity findings above, fix 4.5x->4.15x/76%, and soften the 8-16 threshold. After those corrections the headline conclusion (batching cuts judge cost 30-82% with small, sometimes zero-or-negative accuracy loss) is genuinely backed.

checked.title: Finding 5: Cheap judges recover accuracy via criteria injection + small-model ensembles
checked.note: Fetched https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.13717 ('On Cost-Effective LLM-as-a-Judge Improvement Techniques', submitted Apr 15 2026, v. Jun 9 2026). Abstract verbatim confirms all seven components: four techniques (ensemble scoring, task-specific criteria injection, calibration context, adaptive model escalation); OpenAI GPT + Anthropic Claude families; ensemble + criteria injection reaching 85.8% accuracy / +13.5pp over baseline; criteria injection 'virtually cost free'; 'small models benefit disproportionately from ensembling'; calibration and escalation 'improve over baseline but are dominated by criteria + ensembling on the cost-accuracy Pareto frontier'. CAVEATS: verification is abstract-level only (dataset/task mix, judge sizes, and whether 85.8% is a single-task peak vs average were not inspected); and the final 'Implication' sentence (scaling 12->30 candidates needs criteria + selective self-consistency, not a bigger judge) is the finding-writer's inference, not a result in the paper — keep it labeled as an inference.

overall_note: 4 of 5 findings are genuinely backed by their primary sources, most verified against full text (Findings 1 and 3) or exact abstract quotes (2, 5). Two source-hygiene problems found, both in secondary/search-level citations — exactly where the findings' own confidence labels predicted risk. (1) Finding 1's omega early-stopping rule is NOT in the cited arXiv 2511.00592 (that paper is compiler loop auto-scheduling with no marginal-gain criterion); the rule exists verbatim in SPICE, arXiv 2601.23155 (verified by grepping the PDF: t_stop = min{t : Delta <= omega * first-step Delta}, SPICE+ omega=0.5), but its domain is submodular training-data selection, not agentic loops — re-attribute and re-label. (2) Finding 4's medRxiv sub-claim is number-laundering: the '75.0%->78.2% at batch 15' figure is absent from the actual medRxiv screening PDF (which reports sensitivity/specificity over batches 1-790, never batch 15 or accuracy 75/78.2) and belongs to arXiv 2511.04108, whose batch-15 accuracy is exactly 75.0->78.2; that paper also shows the token collapse is 76% (4.15x), not 4.5x. Finding 4 is marked unsupported AS STATED but is salvageable with the listed corrections; its headline (30.7-82.0% cost cuts at ~3.2pp loss, occasional accuracy gains) survives. Minor precision downgrades elsewhere: HiVid's $8.12 cost baseline is window m=2 (not unbatched), and '+11.5%' is an average-PLCC improvement figure; Finding 2's 'field is abandoning fixed-K' should read 'explicit two-paper precedent exists'. Sources verified only at abstract level: Soccer-GMR (2605.02623), RoBatch (2605.28268), judge-techniques (2604.13717). OpenReview was bot-walled, but the ICLR 2025 GVMR paper was independently confirmed via iclr.cc poster 28243 and the ICLR proceedings.
